input_text:
Launched from the facility at Cape Canaveral, Voyager 1 commenced its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days later, as NASA had assigned specific goals to each of the two probes. The mission, which took off aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a five-year central objective: to observe the outer planets, specifically Jupiter and Saturn, leveraging a rare astronomical alignment of these immense bodies, an event that recurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial high-resolution images of the planet’s faint ring structure and documented the vigorous volcanism on its moon Io, which marked the first recorded instances of such activity originating beyond Earth. Two years afterward, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest passage to Saturn, successfully capturing clear views of its rings and gathering detailed information on Titan. This close flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to leave the ecliptic plane of the solar system, thus concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously commencing its journey into interstellar space.

Following the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the diffused brightness of the Sun, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This marked the last photograph taken by the spacecraft; thereafter, the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to attain this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, holds greetings in 55 languages, selected music, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually discover it. The spacecraft sustains itself using three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose energy supply diminishes due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a speed nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will attain the significant milestone of having traveled one light-day.
output_text:
Launched from the facility located at Cape Canaveral, the Voyager 1 probe embarked on its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 launched sixteen days later, as NASA had assigned distinct objectives to each of the two probes. The mission, which departed aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a primary five-year goal: to observe the outer planets, namely Jupiter and Saturn. This was made possible by a fortuitous celestial alignment of these massive bodies, an occurrence that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this close pass, it transmitted the first high-resolution images showing the planet’s faint ring system and documented the intense volcanism on its moon Io, providing the first recorded evidence of such activity originating beyond Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 conducted its closest flyby of Saturn, successfully obtaining clear visuals of its rings and collecting extensive data regarding Titan. This near encounter with Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart from the ecliptic plane of our solar system, thus concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its voyage into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 oriented its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This particular photograph, which appeared as a solitary pixel against the diffused glare of the Sun, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This represented the final image taken by the spacecraft; afterward, the cameras were deactivated to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a copper phonograph disc plated in gold that measures 12 inches. This disc, developed with input including Sagan’s, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical pieces, and visuals intended for any future civilization that might encounter it. The spacecraft sustains itself using three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose energy source degrades due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers away from Earth, maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, in November 2026, it will reach the important milestone of having traveled one light-day.
